title: Phase 1 Discovery — EMO Day Center Kiosk
description: Complete all discovery questions for the EMO Oregon Day Center kiosk project. Site visit conducted Feb 19, 2026 at the Chautauqua location with Miss Cobine.

title: Q1: Walk me through a typical day at the center, open to close. What time do doors o
description:
Question: Walk me through a typical day at the center, open to close. What time do doors open and close? What happens when someone walks in? Who greets them? Is there a front desk?

Answer: The center has a front entrance with a kiosk area and a back door with a clipboard. Clients sign in at the front entrance. Staff sit in a separate office room within the shelter. The current kiosk is turned off. There is a paper clipboard for sign-in at both entrances. Brochures about additional services and events are on the table near the kiosk. Miss Cobine wants the brochure information displayed on a screen mounted on the wall to free up table space.

Context notes: Need to confirm: hours of operation, who greets clients, peak times.

title: Q2: How do clients currently sign in? Paper log, Excel, nothing? What information is
description:
Question: How do clients currently sign in? Paper log, Excel, nothing? What information is captured at sign-in today? Who handles it — the client or staff?

Answer: Current sign-in: paper clipboard at the front entrance. The existing kiosk hardware (has keyboard and mouse) is turned off and not in use. When the kiosk was active, clients identified themselves from a scrolling list of first name and last initial. This is the UX pattern to carry forward.

Context notes: Key UX constraint: some clients cannot read or write. The system must support BOTH keyboard filtering (type letters of last name to narrow the list) AND scrolling through the full list of names to select. We may iterate on this UX.

title: Q3: How are new clients registered today? What information do you collect on first v
description:
Question: How are new clients registered today? What information do you collect on first visit? Is there an intake form? How long does registration take?

Answer: New clients are directed to the staff office. First day is free — a one-day guest pass. When the client decides to come back for a second day, their record is entered into the system. This is a deliberate workflow: a guest pass before signing up.

Context notes: This is an intentional low-barrier-to-entry design, not a gap. The kiosk should handle 'not found' gracefully — direct client to staff office for first visit. Registration happens in the staff office, not at the kiosk.

title: Q4: What services does the center offer? Which services need to be tracked per visit
description:
Question: What services does the center offer? Which services need to be tracked per visit? Are any services limited (e.g., one shower per day)?

Answer: Services include showers and laundry (time-slotted). Other services mentioned via brochures on the table. Full service list still needed. Nice-to-have: client picks an available time for shower or laundry at check-in. This goes into a schedule and notifies staff.

Context notes: Have printout sheets (examples of sign-up sheets for services) from the visit. Need to photograph/digitize these. Shower and laundry scheduling is a Phase 2 nice-to-have — build check-in first, then add scheduling.

title: Q5: How many clients per day, roughly? Average day? Busiest day? How many total uniq
description:
Question: How many clients per day, roughly? Average day? Busiest day? How many total unique clients over a year?

Helper: Sizing data. Affects database design, scroll list length, and performance requirements.

title: Q6: Who are the staff members who will use this system? How many staff? What roles?
description:
Question: Who are the staff members who will use this system? How many staff? What roles? Volunteers? Who is the day-to-day system owner?

Answer: Staff office has 4 desks. Miss Cobine has a desktop and a laptop. All other staff have laptops which they take home. Miss Cobine is the primary contact and decision maker.

Context notes: Prior IT person was let go — concerns were speed of work and reluctance to multitask. Tasks included laptop setup, printer/fax, and software development. The expectation is efficient use of time — e.g., start another task while waiting for a laptop to download updates.

title: Q7: What reports do you run today? Daily counts? Monthly summaries? Who reads these
description:
Question: What reports do you run today? Daily counts? Monthly summaries? Who reads these reports (board, funders, staff)? What format (printed, emailed, uploaded)?

Helper: Reports are the output of the system. If we don't know what comes out, we can't design what goes in.

title: Q8: What are your funding sources? HUD homeless assistance grants (triggers HMIS)? R
description:
Question: What are your funding sources? HUD homeless assistance grants (triggers HMIS)? Ryan White HIV/AIDS Program (triggers RSR)? SAMHSA? State or county contracts? Private foundations?

Helper: Funding sources dictate reporting requirements. This is the most consequential question for system design.

title: Q9: Do you currently report to HMIS? Which regional CoC? Which HMIS vendor (Clarity,
description:
Question: Do you currently report to HMIS? Which regional CoC? Which HMIS vendor (Clarity, ServicePoint, other)? Who does the data entry? How and how often?

Helper: If they report to HMIS, we need to understand the current workflow so we can export compatible data.

title: Q10: Do you report Ryan White / RSR data? Do you use CAREWare? Who handles RSR report
description:
Question: Do you report Ryan White / RSR data? Do you use CAREWare? Who handles RSR reporting?

Helper: Ryan White reporting has specific data requirements that affect client data fields.

title: Q11: What other reporting obligations exist? Funder-specific reports? Internal board
description:
Question: What other reporting obligations exist? Funder-specific reports? Internal board reports? County or state reporting?

Helper: Catch-all for any reporting requirements not covered by HMIS or RSR.

title: Q12: Is there existing data to migrate? Excel spreadsheets? Access database? How far
description:
Question: Is there existing data to migrate? Excel spreadsheets? Access database? How far back? How clean is it? Can I get a copy (de-identified if needed)?

Helper: Data migration scope affects timeline and cost. Dirty data = more work.

title: Q13: What demographic fields are needed at registration? For each: is it required by
description:
Question: What demographic fields are needed at registration? For each: is it required by a funder? (First/last name, preferred name, DOB, SSN, gender identity, race, ethnicity, veteran status, disabling condition, prior living situation)

Helper: Start with the minimum. For each field ask: Do you need this? Is it required by a funder? Fewer fields = faster registration = fewer errors.

title: Q14: What contact and program-specific fields are needed? (Phone, email, emergency co
description:
Question: What contact and program-specific fields are needed? (Phone, email, emergency contact, insurance status, income level, household composition, referral source)

Helper: Contact info may not be available for all clients. Consider which fields are truly necessary vs nice-to-have.

title: Q15: HIV status collection: Does your grant reporting require tracking HIV status at
description:
Question: HIV status collection: Does your grant reporting require tracking HIV status at the individual client level? Or does the center serve people who may be living with HIV without recording that specifically?

Helper: Most sensitive data question. Options: (a) Don't collect it — safest, nothing to protect. (b) Collect as yes/no for grant compliance. (c) Collect with detail. Recommendation: option (a) unless a funder explicitly requires it. Under ORS 433.045, HIV data has heightened confidentiality protections.

title: Q16: What operational fields are needed? Photo for ID/recognition? Client ID card iss
description:
Question: What operational fields are needed? Photo for ID/recognition? Client ID card issued? Notes/comments field? Case manager assignment?

Helper: Photos add complexity (storage, privacy). ID cards add a physical process. Notes fields are useful but need access control.

title: Q17: What check-in fields should be captured at each visit? (Services used, check-out
description:
Question: What check-in fields should be captured at each visit? (Services used, check-out time, notes, reason for visit, referrals made)

Helper: Date/time is automatic. Services come from the service list. Consider: does check-out happen (manual or auto at close)?

title: Q18: Describe the physical layout of the center. Is there a front desk or reception a
description:
Question: Describe the physical layout of the center. Is there a front desk or reception area? Where would clients check in? Where do staff sit? Is the check-in area in line of sight of staff?

Answer: The center has:
- A front entrance with the current (inactive) kiosk and sign-in table
- A back door with a clipboard for sign-in
- A separate staff office room (not in line of sight of check-in)
- The staff office has 4 desks
- Miss Cobine has a desktop at her desk plus a laptop
- All other staff have laptops which they take home with them
- The kiosk table currently has brochures about services and events
- Kitchen area had WiFi coverage issues, recently mitigated with additional AP

Context notes: Staff office is SEPARATE from the check-in area — this is Topology 2. The kiosk must operate independently. Staff access to the system is from their office.

title: Q19: Is there a desk where both a client screen and a staff screen could sit side by
description:
Question: Is there a desk where both a client screen and a staff screen could sit side by side (Topology 1)? Or does staff admin need to be in a separate room from client check-in (Topology 2)?

Answer: Topology 2: Staff admin is in a separate room from client check-in. The staff office has 4 desks and is not in the same room as the front entrance kiosk.

Context notes: This means: standalone kiosk at front entrance, staff access via their office computers (laptops/desktop). Requires either network connectivity between kiosk and staff machines, or the staff interface runs on the same machine and they remote in / use a web interface.

title: Q20: Power situation: Are there outlets at the check-in location? Is the power reliab
description:
Question: Power situation: Are there outlets at the check-in location? Is the power reliable? Surges or outages? Does the center have a UPS or surge protection?

Helper: A UPS is cheap insurance. If power is unreliable, factor this into hardware choice (tablet with battery = free UPS).

title: Q21: Internet: Is there WiFi at the center? The kiosk does NOT need internet for dail
description:
Question: Internet: Is there WiFi at the center? The kiosk does NOT need internet for daily operation. But is occasional internet available for software updates? Or is the system truly air-gapped?

Answer: Wireless internet is provided to clients. Comcast business router is visible in the office. Bundles of Cat 5 cabling go in various directions from the office. The kitchen had WiFi coverage issues — a recent addition was an additional WiFi access point. Internet is available for the kiosk if needed.

Context notes: WiFi is available and functional. The kiosk could use the network for staff notifications and real-time data access from the staff office. This opens up a web-based architecture where the kiosk runs a local web app and staff access it over the LAN.

title: Q22: Security of the device: Is the check-in area supervised at all times? Risk of th
description:
Question: Security of the device: Is the check-in area supervised at all times? Risk of theft or tampering? Does the device need to be locked down or bolted?

Helper: Wall-mounted screen is inherently more secure than a device on a table. Consider cable locks, VESA mounts, tamper-resistant enclosures.

title: Q23: Single screen or dual screen? Single: staff and clients share one screen with PI
description:
Question: Single screen or dual screen? Single: staff and clients share one screen with PIN mode switch. Dual: client screen + staff screen (recommended if there's a front desk).

Answer: Wall-mounted touchscreen for client check-in. Staff access from their office (separate room). Not a dual-screen-at-one-desk scenario. When asked about preference, the room chose touchscreen over mouse/keyboard.

Context notes: Miss Cobine asked the room: mouse or touchscreen? Only reply was touchscreen. This is a single client-facing touchscreen on the wall, with staff accessing the system from their own devices in the office.

title: Q24: Hardware tier preference? Tier 1: All-in-One touchscreen PC (~$400-700). Tier 2:
description:
Question: Hardware tier preference? Tier 1: All-in-One touchscreen PC (~$400-700). Tier 2: Windows tablet + locking stand (~$300-600). Tier 3: Mini PC + touchscreen monitor (~$350-600).

Helper: Miss Cobine wants a wall-mounted screen. This favors Tier 3 (mini PC hidden + wall-mounted touch monitor) or a wall-mountable AIO.

title: Q25: New or refurbished hardware? Refurbished business AIO: ~$250-350. New: ~$400-700
description:
Question: New or refurbished hardware? Refurbished business AIO: ~$250-350. New: ~$400-700.

Helper: Refurb saves money but shorter warranty. For a mission-critical kiosk, new with 3-year warranty may be worth it.

title: Q26: Do you want spare hardware on hand? A second mini PC or spare AIO means same-day
description:
Question: Do you want spare hardware on hand? A second mini PC or spare AIO means same-day recovery. Without it, recovery depends on sourcing a replacement.

Helper: USB backup drive + install media can restore the system onto ANY Windows 11 Pro PC. Spare hardware is insurance.

title: Q27: Software path: C# native Windows app or Python/Flask via WSL2?
description:
Question: Software path: C# native Windows app or Python/Flask via WSL2?

Helper: Internal decision — may not need client input. C# recommended for fewer moving parts and more reliable startup. Python if portability to Pi matters or developer is significantly more productive in Python.

title: Q28: How many staff accounts are needed? What permission levels make sense? (Proposed
description:
Question: How many staff accounts are needed? What permission levels make sense? (Proposed: Admin, Staff, Kiosk)

Helper: Proposed roles: Admin (full access, user management, settings), Staff (register clients, view records, run reports), Kiosk (client-facing check-in only, no login required).

title: Q29: How should the client check-in screen work? (a) Client enters a code from an ID
description:
Question: How should the client check-in screen work? (a) Client enters a code from an ID card, (b) Client types their name and selects from matches, (c) Client enters DOB + last name, (d) Staff checks them in (not self-service).

Answer: Client identifies from a scrolling list of first name and last initial. This was the previous kiosk workflow and should carry forward. Must also support typing letters of a last name to filter down the list, because some clients cannot read or write and need to scroll visually. Both input methods required: type-to-filter AND scroll-to-select.

Context notes: Critical accessibility requirement: some clients cannot read or write. The scrolling list with first name + last initial is a proven UX for this population. Type-to-filter is an enhancement for literate clients, not a replacement for scrolling.

title: Q30: Can clients self-register, or must staff do it? What should happen if someone is
description:
Question: Can clients self-register, or must staff do it? What should happen if someone isn't in the system?

Answer: Clients do NOT self-register. New clients are directed to the staff office. First day is free (guest pass). Registration enters the system on second visit. If someone isn't in the system, the kiosk should say: please see staff.

Context notes: Guest pass / free first day is a deliberate low-barrier design. Registration requires staff involvement — collected in the office, not at the kiosk.

title: Q31: Data retention: 10-year unified policy? (Satisfies Oregon medical, HIPAA, HMIS,
description:
Question: Data retention: 10-year unified policy? (Satisfies Oregon medical, HIPAA, HMIS, federal grants.) Confirm or adjust.

Helper: 10 years covers the most conservative requirement. Shorter is simpler but may miss a grant obligation.

title: Q32: Backup rotation: weekly swap (Drive A/B) + annual archive (Drive C)? Confirm or
description:
Question: Backup rotation: weekly swap (Drive A/B) + annual archive (Drive C)? Confirm or adjust.

Helper: Simple, physical, air-gapped. Staff swaps USB drives weekly. Annual drive goes in a safe.

title: Q33: Open source or proprietary code? Open source (MIT): reusable, no lock-in, commun
description:
Question: Open source or proprietary code? Open source (MIT): reusable, no lock-in, community. Proprietary: EMO owns exclusively.

Helper: Recommendation is open source. Benefits other shelters. No competitive disadvantage for EMO.

title: Q34: Who holds the database decryption password? Which staff member(s)? Where is it s
description:
Question: Who holds the database decryption password? Which staff member(s)? Where is it stored (sealed envelope in a safe)? Who is the backup if that person leaves?

Helper: Key custody is an organizational decision. Needs at least two people who know the password. Written backup in a sealed envelope in a safe is standard.

title: Q35: Data incident procedure: Does EMO have an existing data breach policy? If a USB
description:
Question: Data incident procedure: Does EMO have an existing data breach policy? If a USB drive is lost, who gets notified?

Helper: Encrypted USB drives mitigate breach impact. But the policy should exist before it's needed.

title: Q36: When do you want the system operational? Is there a hard deadline (grant cycle,
description:
Question: When do you want the system operational? Is there a hard deadline (grant cycle, fiscal year)? Or 'as soon as practical'?

Helper: Hard deadlines constrain everything. 'As soon as practical' gives room to do it right.

title: Q37: Hardware ordering: Does EMO purchase directly, or do I order and invoice? Who ap
description:
Question: Hardware ordering: Does EMO purchase directly, or do I order and invoice? Who approves the purchase? Ship to the center or to me for setup?

Helper: Shipping to developer for setup means the system arrives ready to install. Shipping to center means one fewer handoff.

title: Q38: Development access: Who is my primary point of contact at EMO? Best way to reach
description:
Question: Development access: Who is my primary point of contact at EMO? Best way to reach them for questions during build?

Answer: Miss Cobine is the primary point of contact at EMO.

Context notes: Met at the shelter location on Feb 19, 2026. She led the meeting and made decisions.

title: Q39: Training: How many staff need training? On-site training at deployment? Written
description:
Question: Training: How many staff need training? On-site training at deployment? Written guide sufficient for ongoing reference?

Helper: Plan for on-site training at go-live plus a written guide. Consider staff turnover — new hires need to learn too.

title: Q40: Testing: Can I deploy a test version at the center before going live? Is there a
description:
Question: Testing: Can I deploy a test version at the center before going live? Is there a quiet period to do a soft launch?

Helper: A week of parallel running (kiosk + paper clipboard) builds confidence before cutting over.

title: Q41: Wall-mounted display preference: Miss Cobine wants the screen mounted on the wal
description:
Question: Wall-mounted display preference: Miss Cobine wants the screen mounted on the wall to free up table space. What size? What height? Does the wall location have power and line-of-sight to staff?

Answer: Miss Cobine wants the screen mounted on the wall to free up table space. This replaces the current table-based kiosk setup. Touchscreen confirmed as preferred input.

Context notes: Wall mount changes hardware calculus: need VESA-compatible touchscreen + hidden mini PC, or a wall-mountable AIO. Consider ergonomics: height for standing clients, angle for touch interaction. ADA accessibility if required.

title: Q42: Information radiator: What events, calendar items, schedules, and brochure conte
description:
Question: Information radiator: What events, calendar items, schedules, and brochure content should display on the kiosk screen when not in use for check-in?

Answer: Miss Cobine wants the kiosk screen to display events, calendar, schedule, and brochure content when not in use for check-in. Currently this information is on paper brochures on the table. Digital display would free up table space and keep information current.

Context notes: This is an idle-mode / screensaver feature. Needs a staff-friendly way to update content (events, calendar, announcements). Could be as simple as a folder of images or a lightweight CMS. The screen cycles through content until a client taps to check in.

title: Q43: Shower and laundry scheduling: Should clients be able to pick an available time
description:
Question: Shower and laundry scheduling: Should clients be able to pick an available time slot for showers or laundry at check-in? How does this notify staff? How many slots per time period?

Answer: Nice-to-have: clients pick an available time slot for showers or laundry at check-in. The booking goes into a schedule and notifies staff. Have printout sheets showing current paper-based sign-up process.

Context notes: Nice-to-have, not MVP. Build check-in and information radiator first. Scheduling adds: time slot config, availability display, booking logic, staff notifications. Good Phase 2 feature.

title: Q8: What are your funding sources? HUD homeless assistance grants (triggers HMIS)? R
description:
**Confirmed funding sources and reporting obligations:**

- **Ryan White HIV/AIDS Program** — Active. RSR reporting via Multnomah County Health Department (.multnomah.co.us CAREWare instance). Julie uploads counts to CAREWare daily. Annual RSR submission assumed.
- **CACFP (Child and Adult Care Food Program)** — Active. Quarterly reporting. Drives meal tracking requirements.
- **Additional funding sources** — At least "a few" with annual reporting. Details not yet captured.

**Pending confirmation:**
- **HMIS** — Not recognized by Chautauqua staff. Confirm with Andrea whether EMO reports to HMIS and which CoC/vendor.
- **JOH** — Quarterly reporting mentioned. Full name and requirements TBD. Can defer until RSR preliminary reporting is complete.

**Integration points (future phases):**
- HOP Fastpass (TriMet) — distribution tracking
- At least one other service TBD
- These are potential Phase 2+ integration points if time allows.

**Key implication:** Ryan White / CAREWare is the primary external data dependency. The kiosk data model must produce CAREWare-compatible output. HMIS compatibility is contingent on Andrea's answer.

metadata.notes:
FOLLOW-UP ITEMS:
1. Andrea — confirm HMIS participation (which CoC, which vendor)
2. JOH — get full name and reporting requirements (defer until RSR preliminary done)
3. Annual reports — get details on additional funding sources
4. HOP Fastpass + other integration points — catalog for Phase 2 scoping
5. Printout numbering: Q2.4 on old printout = CAREWare/daily uploads (now Q10 in current config)

title: Q8: What are your funding sources? HUD homeless assistance grants (triggers HMIS)? R
description:
**Confirmed funding sources and reporting obligations:**

- **Ryan White HIV/AIDS Program** — Active. RSR reporting via Multnomah County Health Department (.multnomah.co.us CAREWare instance). Julie uploads counts to CAREWare daily. Annual RSR submission assumed.
- **CACFP (Child and Adult Care Food Program)** — Active. Quarterly reporting. Drives meal tracking requirements.
- **Additional funding sources** — At least "a few" with annual reporting. Details not yet captured.

**Pending confirmation:**
- **HMIS** — Not recognized by Chautauqua staff. Confirm with Andrea whether EMO reports to HMIS and which CoC/vendor.
- **JOH** — Quarterly reporting mentioned. Full name and requirements TBD. Can defer until RSR preliminary reporting is complete.

**Integration points (future phases):**

- At least one other service TBD
- These are potential Phase 2+ integration points if time allows.

**Key implication:** Ryan White / CAREWare is the primary external data dependency. The kiosk data model must produce CAREWare-compatible output. HMIS compatibility is contingent on Andrea's answer.

FOLLOW-UP ITEMS:

2. JOH — get full name and reporting requirements (defer until RSR preliminary done)
3. Annual reports — get details on additional funding sources
4. HOP Fastpass + other integration points — catalog for Phase 2 scoping
5. Printout numbering: Q2.4 on old printout = CAREWare/daily uploads (now Q10 in current config)

title: Q10: Do you report Ryan White / RSR data? Do you use CAREWare? Who handles RSR report
description:
Julie uploads counts to CAREWare daily via the Multnomah County Health Department instance (.multnomah.co.us). RSR reporting is active. Annual RSR submission assumed.

Details on CAREWare workflow (which fields Julie enters, how data is currently gathered) still needed.

metadata.notes: Partial from Q8 session. Julie is the key person for CAREWare workflow details. Need to understand what she enters daily so we can match or automate from kiosk data.

title: Q12: Is there existing data to migrate? Excel spreadsheets? Access database? How far
description: Existing data migration scope TBD. Andrea is the contact for understanding current data systems. Appointment to meet her (in person or virtual) in the next day or so.

metadata.notes: Andrea meeting is the next action item. She likely knows about HMIS status too (from Q8 follow-up).

title: Q6: Who are the staff members who will use this system? How many staff? What roles?
description:
Approximately 8 staff members will use the system. Staff office has 4 desks. Miss Cobine (CC) has a desktop and is the day-to-day system owner with admin access.

Additionally, rotating nursing students and a teacher augment shelter staff on approximately 6-week rotations. These students need sufficient access to enter notes about their contact/services with clients. They are not permanent staff but need write access to client records during their rotation.

metadata.notes: Student/volunteer role is a new requirement not in the original 3-tier proposal. Need a role between Staff and Kiosk — or just use Staff with time-limited accounts that CC can create/disable.

title: Q28: How many staff accounts are needed? What permission levels make sense? (Proposed
description:
Approximately 8 staff accounts needed. Proposed 3-tier roles (Admin, Staff, Kiosk) accepted as a starting point.

- **Admin**: CC (Miss Cobine) — must be able to create and disable accounts. Full system access.
- **Staff**: Regular shelter staff — register clients, add notes about points of contact and services, view records, run reports.
- **Student/Volunteer**: Rotating nursing students and a teacher on ~6-week rotations. Need access to enter notes per client about their contact for services. CC creates accounts at rotation start, disables at rotation end.
- **Kiosk**: Client-facing check-in screen, no login required.

Account lifecycle: CC creates accounts for new staff/students, disables when they leave or rotation ends.

metadata.notes: May need a 4th role (Student) or just use Staff with time-limited accounts. Key point: CC is the account administrator.

title: Q16: What operational fields are needed? Photo for ID/recognition? Client ID card iss
description:
Operational fields for client records:

- **Photos**: Optional. Not required for MVP.
- **"Seen before" indicator**: Client records must show whether a person has had prior contact with the center. This is a returning-client flag, not detailed history — just "have we seen this person before."
- **Concern flag**: A flag on client records when there is a concern. Details of the concern system TBD, but the record needs to indicate "flagged" status visible at check-in.
- **Notes/comments**: Staff and rotating students add notes about points of contact and services for each client. This is a core workflow — not optional.
- **Case manager assignment**: TBD.

metadata.notes: The "seen before" + concern flag combination means the check-in screen should surface this info when a returning client checks in. Staff/students need to see flags without needing to dig into records.

title: Q29: How should the client check-in screen work? (a) Client enters a code from an ID
description:
Current/prior system: scrolling list of last name + first initial. Client selects themselves from the list.

For the kiosk, start with this proven pattern — scrollable list with keyboard filtering. Support both scrolling the full list AND typing to filter. May iterate on UX after initial deployment.

Deferred (not Phase 1): QR codes, ID cards/badges, phone app. Stay focused on valuable deliverables first.

metadata.notes: The lastname+firstinitial scroll is what clients already know. Changing it adds friction. Build this first, iterate later. QR/badge/app are Phase 2+ at earliest.

title: Q43: Shower and laundry scheduling: Should clients be able to pick an available time
description:
Shower and laundry are limited availability. Clients sign up at check-in for a first-come-first-served spot in the daily calendar. This is a check-in workflow feature, not a separate scheduling system.

At check-in, the kiosk should show available slots and let the client claim one. Once slots are full, the option is unavailable for that day.

metadata.notes: This reinforces Q4 (services with limits). The sign-up-at-checkin model is simpler than a separate scheduling UI. Just append slot selection to the check-in flow.

title: Q4: What services does the center offer? Which services need to be tracked per visit
description:
Services include showers and laundry (limited availability, first-come-first-served daily sign-up at check-in), meals (tracked for CACFP quarterly reporting), and additional services TBD.

Shower and laundry slots are claimed during check-in. Once daily slots are full, unavailable for that day. Meals must be counted for CACFP compliance.

Full service list still needed from CC/staff.

metadata.notes: CACFP from Q8 confirms meals are a funded/tracked service. Shower/laundry scheduling confirmed as check-in flow feature.

title: Q12: Is there existing data to migrate? Excel spreadsheets? Access database? How far
description: Existing data migration scope TBD. Andrea is the contact for understanding current data systems. Appointment to meet her (in person or virtual) in the next day or so.

metadata.notes: Andrea meeting is the next action item. She likely knows about HMIS status too (from Q8 follow-up).

title: Q6: Who are the staff members who will use this system? How many staff? What roles?
description:
Approximately 8 staff members will use the system. Staff office has 4 desks. Miss Cobine (CC) has a desktop and is the day-to-day system owner with admin access.

Additionally, rotating nursing students and a teacher augment shelter staff on approximately 6-week rotations. These students need sufficient access to enter notes about their contact/services with clients. They are not permanent staff but need write access to client records during their rotation.

metadata.notes: Student/volunteer role is a new requirement not in the original 3-tier proposal. Need a role between Staff and Kiosk — or just use Staff with time-limited accounts that CC can create/disable.

title: Q28: How many staff accounts are needed? What permission levels make sense? (Proposed
description:
Approximately 8 staff accounts needed. Proposed 3-tier roles (Admin, Staff, Kiosk) accepted as a starting point.

- **Admin**: CC (Miss Cobine) — must be able to create and disable accounts. Full system access.
- **Staff**: Regular shelter staff — register clients, add notes about points of contact and services, view records, run reports.
- **Student/Volunteer**: Rotating nursing students and a teacher on ~6-week rotations. Need access to enter notes per client about their contact for services. CC creates accounts at rotation start, disables at rotation end.
- **Kiosk**: Client-facing check-in screen, no login required.

Account lifecycle: CC creates accounts for new staff/students, disables when they leave or rotation ends.

metadata.notes: May need a 4th role (Student) or just use Staff with time-limited accounts. Key point: CC is the account administrator.

title: Q16: What operational fields are needed? Photo for ID/recognition? Client ID card iss
description:
Operational fields for client records:

- **Photos**: Optional. Not required for MVP.
- **"Seen before" indicator**: Client records must show whether a person has had prior contact with the center. This is a returning-client flag, not detailed history — just "have we seen this person before."
- **Concern flag**: A flag on client records when there is a concern. Details of the concern system TBD, but the record needs to indicate "flagged" status visible at check-in.
- **Notes/comments**: Staff and rotating students add notes about points of contact and services for each client. This is a core workflow — not optional.
- **Case manager assignment**: TBD.

metadata.notes: The "seen before" + concern flag combination means the check-in screen should surface this info when a returning client checks in. Staff/students need to see flags without needing to dig into records.

title: Q29: How should the client check-in screen work? (a) Client enters a code from an ID
description:
Current/prior system: scrolling list of last name + first initial. Client selects themselves from the list.

For the kiosk, start with this proven pattern — scrollable list with keyboard filtering. Support both scrolling the full list AND typing to filter. May iterate on UX after initial deployment.

Deferred (not Phase 1): QR codes, ID cards/badges, phone app. Stay focused on valuable deliverables first.

metadata.notes: The lastname+firstinitial scroll is what clients already know. Changing it adds friction. Build this first, iterate later. QR/badge/app are Phase 2+ at earliest.

title: Q43: Shower and laundry scheduling: Should clients be able to pick an available time
description:
Shower and laundry are limited availability. Clients sign up at check-in for a first-come-first-served spot in the daily calendar. This is a check-in workflow feature, not a separate scheduling system.

At check-in, the kiosk should show available slots and let the client claim one. Once slots are full, the option is unavailable for that day.

metadata.notes: This reinforces Q4 (services with limits). The sign-up-at-checkin model is simpler than a separate scheduling UI. Just append slot selection to the check-in flow.

title: Q4: What services does the center offer? Which services need to be tracked per visit
description:
Services include showers and laundry (limited availability, first-come-first-served daily sign-up at check-in), meals (tracked for CACFP quarterly reporting), and additional services TBD.

Shower and laundry slots are claimed during check-in. Once daily slots are full, unavailable for that day. Meals must be counted for CACFP compliance.

Full service list still needed from CC/staff.

metadata.notes: CACFP from Q8 confirms meals are a funded/tracked service. Shower/laundry scheduling confirmed as check-in flow feature.

title: Q42: Information radiator: What events, calendar items, schedules, and brochure conte
description:
Miss Cobine wants the kiosk screen to display events, calendar items, service schedules, and brochure-style content when not in use for check-in (idle/screensaver mode). Replaces physical table brochures with digital content. Staff need a way to update this content.

The calendar/events data should be syndicated as a standard feed (iCal/webcal) so it can also be subscribed to from a mobile app. Reference model: Trap Door Brewing events calendar — maintain in one place, display everywhere. Kiosk idle screen is one output, mobile subscription is another.

metadata.notes: Calendar syndication is low-cost if we use a standard format from the start. iCal feed means any phone can subscribe. Could also power a simple web page later. This is not a separate feature — it is how the data is stored (standard calendar format) rather than extra work.

title: Q42: Information radiator: What events, calendar items, schedules, and brochure conte
description:
Miss Cobine wants the kiosk screen to display events, calendar items, service schedules, and brochure-style content when not in use for check-in (idle/screensaver mode). Replaces physical table brochures with digital content. Staff need a way to update this content.

The calendar/events data should be syndicated as a standard feed (iCal/webcal) so it can also be subscribed to from a mobile app. Reference model: Trap Door Brewing events calendar — maintain in one place, display everywhere. Kiosk idle screen is one output, mobile subscription is another.

metadata.notes: Calendar syndication is low-cost if we use a standard format from the start. iCal feed means any phone can subscribe. Could also power a simple web page later. This is not a separate feature — it is how the data is stored (standard calendar format) rather than extra work.

title: Q24: Hardware tier preference? Tier 1: All-in-One touchscreen PC (~$400-700). Tier 2:
description:
Recommendation: durable touchscreen display (wall-mounted per CC preference) with a separate mini PC. This is Tier 3 (mini PC + touchscreen monitor).

The mini PC can be hidden behind the mount or in a nearby cabinet. Touchscreen should be commercial/durable grade for the client-facing environment.

metadata.notes: Wall mount + durability are the driving requirements. Tier 3 is the natural fit. Tier 1 AIO could also work if VESA-mountable and sufficiently rugged.

title: Q21: Internet: Is there WiFi at the center? The kiosk does NOT need internet for dail
description:
The kiosk does NOT need to be air-gapped. There is a local WiFi network at the center and staff have access. The kiosk can connect to WiFi for software updates, data sync, and potential future integrations.

Network architecture consideration: a dedicated VLAN or software-defined network segment for the kiosk is recommended if the existing WiFi gear supports it (most business-grade APs do). This isolates kiosk traffic from staff devices as a defense-in-depth measure. One-time setup, no ongoing complexity. If consumer-grade gear, skip — not worth a hardware upgrade for this alone.

metadata.notes: SDN/VLAN is a "if the gear supports it" recommendation, not a hard requirement. The real security is at the application layer (auth, encryption). Network segmentation is bonus.

title: Q18: Describe the physical layout of the center. Is there a front desk or reception a
description:
Staff office has 4 desks with laptops. Miss Cobine (CC) has an additional desktop in an office within the shelter — this is a separate room from the client area.

The kiosk (wall-mounted touchscreen) would be in the client-facing area. Staff access the system from their laptops over local WiFi. CC's desktop could serve as the database host if a separate server device is not warranted.

Topology confirmed (Q19): separate rooms — client kiosk in common area, staff admin in office. Connected via local WiFi network.

metadata.notes: CC desktop as database host is the simplest topology — no extra hardware for a server. Kiosk connects to CC desktop over WiFi. Staff laptops also connect. All on same LAN (or VLAN-segmented if gear supports it).

title: Q22: Security of the device: Is the check-in area supervised at all times? Risk of th
description:
The kiosk will be a wall-mounted durable touchscreen in the client area. Wall mounting inherently reduces theft risk (no loose device on a table). The check-in area is within staff line of sight from the office.

Network security: local WiFi with username/password authentication on services. Optionally, a dedicated VLAN for the kiosk to isolate it from staff devices (defense in depth). Application-layer security (role-based auth, encrypted database) is the primary protection.

Physical security measures: VESA wall mount (tamper-resistant screws recommended), mini PC in a locked cabinet or behind the mount.

metadata.notes: Defense in depth layers: (1) physical — wall mount, (2) network — VLAN if supported, (3) application — role-based auth + encryption. Don't over-engineer but don't skip free wins.

title: Q31: Data retention: 10-year unified policy? (Satisfies Oregon medical, HIPAA, HMIS,
description:
Data retention details deferred to Andrea. CC's sense is 10-12 year retention, which aligns with the proposed 10-year unified policy.

Retention policy should include:
- Annual offsite backup (physical media stored off-premises)
- More frequent backups as required by specific grants
- Documented process with system-generated reminders alerting staff of what to do and when (e.g., "time to rotate backup drives", "annual archive due")

Final policy confirmation pending Andrea meeting.

metadata.notes: CC agrees with the ballpark. Andrea will confirm against specific grant requirements. The system-generated reminders are a nice touch — make compliance automatic rather than relying on memory.

title: Q32: Backup rotation: weekly swap (Drive A/B) + annual archive (Drive C)? Confirm or
description:
Annual offsite backup confirmed. More frequent backups as required, with documented process.

Key requirement from CC: the system itself should alert staff with reminders of what to do and when — backup rotation should not depend on staff remembering. Built-in reminders/notifications for backup tasks.

Specific rotation schedule (weekly A/B swap vs other) TBD with Andrea.

metadata.notes: System-generated backup reminders are a feature requirement. Could be as simple as a dashboard notification or as formal as an email/alert.

title: Q37: Hardware ordering: Does EMO purchase directly, or do I order and invoice? Who ap
description:
EMO purchases hardware directly — we do NOT purchase on their behalf. Express Pros (middle layer consulting firm) declined to handle hardware purchasing due to warranty complications.

EMO currently has Dell laptops and shops at Costco. We will provide a shopping cart or specific URLs/links for recommended hardware so EMO can purchase directly through their preferred channels (Dell.com, Costco).

This simplifies warranty: EMO owns the hardware, warranty is in their name, no middleman.

metadata.notes: Express Pros is a consulting layer between us and EMO. They handle non-technical coordination. Hardware warranty goes directly to EMO. Our deliverable is a hardware recommendation list with purchase links.

title: Q23: Single screen or dual screen? Single: staff and clients share one screen with PI
description:
The system scope is broader than a single kiosk screen. The architecture serves:

1. **Client kiosk**: Wall-mounted durable touchscreen AIO for self-service check-in (known clients)
2. **Staff laptops**: Existing Dell laptops used by staff to enter data, manage records, run reports — accessed via browser over local WiFi
3. **CC desktop**: Admin access + potential database host

So the "screen" question is really about the client-facing kiosk hardware. Staff already have their screens (laptops). The software runs as a local web app accessible from any device on the network.

metadata.notes: This reframes the architecture: it is a local web application, not just a kiosk app. The kiosk is one client. Staff laptops are other clients. CC desktop is the server. All over local WiFi.

title: Q25: New or refurbished hardware? Refurbished business AIO: ~$250-350. New: ~$400-700
description:
New hardware preferred. EMO purchases through Dell or Costco (existing vendor relationships). Warranty stays in EMO's name.

For the kiosk AIO: need a durable touchscreen, ideally Dell (brand consistency with their laptops) or available at Costco. Commercial/business-grade preferred over consumer for durability in a day center environment.

metadata.notes: Costco has good return policy which partially offsets warranty concerns. Dell business line (OptiPlex AIO) would be ideal if available with touchscreen.

title: Q41: Wall-mounted display preference: Miss Cobine wants the screen mounted on the wal
description:
CC wants the client kiosk screen wall-mounted to free up table space. Needs to be a durable touchscreen AIO, VESA-mountable.

Hardware sourcing: EMO shops at Dell and Costco. Need to identify a specific durable touchscreen AIO from one of these vendors that is VESA-compatible and suitable for a day center environment (frequent use by many hands, needs to withstand daily wear).

Size, height, and exact wall location TBD during installation planning. Power availability at wall location needs confirmation.

metadata.notes: ACTION: Research Dell touchscreen AIO options (OptiPlex AIO with touch?) and Costco availability. Provide EMO with specific product links/shopping cart.

- Documented process with system-generated reminders alerting staff of what to do and when (e.g., "time to rotate backup drives", "annual archive due")

title: Q36: When do you want the system operational? Is there a hard deadline (grant cycle,
description:
Timeline TBD — pending Andrea meeting and hardware ordering decisions.

Potential scope expansion (Phase 2+ only, contingent on ahead of schedule AND under budget):
- Light IT services for this location and possibly a second shelter location CC mentioned
- NetBox deployment on the server for hardware asset tracking (who has what) and network documentation
- This would give EMO a proper inventory and network documentation layer across locations

Phase 1 remains: kiosk + staff web app + CAREWare-compatible data. No scope creep.

metadata.notes: STRICT GATE: Phase 2 items only if Phase 1 is delivered ahead of schedule and under budget. NetBox is open source and lightweight — low cost to add if the server is already running. Second shelter location multiplies value of the whole system.

title: Q36: When do you want the system operational? Is there a hard deadline (grant cycle,

title: Q24: Hardware tier preference? Tier 1: All-in-One touchscreen PC (~$400-700). Tier 2:
description:
Hardware decision corrected back to existing Pi-based research. See Recommended_System_A1.md and Meeting_Notes_Hardware_Options.md for full BOM with purchase links.

**Kiosk (table, check-in):** Pi 5 + touchscreen + enclosure (KKSB or Waveshare CM5). Existing BOM applies. ~$315-390 depending on path. Sourced from Adafruit, Amazon, B&H Photo — NOT Costco (they don't carry Pi).

**Info radiator (wall, calendar/events):** Pi Zero 2 W (~$20) + HDMI to a wall-mounted screen. The screen could be a cheap TV from Costco (~$100-150 for a 32"). The Pi runs Chromium in kiosk mode pointed at the server's /calendar endpoint.

**Server:** Either a dedicated Pi 5 in CC's office or software on CC's existing desktop. Decision pending — see Q27.

**Staff access:** Existing Dell laptops via browser over WiFi. No new hardware needed.

EMO purchases the wall TV from Costco/Dell (familiar vendors). Pi hardware sourced per existing BOM from Adafruit/Amazon. We provide the shopping cart links.

metadata.notes: AIO option was a distraction. Existing Pi research in Meeting_Notes_Hardware_Options.md is thorough and correct. The new element is the info radiator as a separate wall display — Pi Zero 2 W is the cheapest consistent option.

title: Q27: Software path: C# native Windows app or Python/Flask via WSL2?
description:
Python/Flask web app. No WSL2, no C# native.

The architecture is a local web application served over WiFi:
- **Server**: Flask + SQLite on a Pi 5 (dedicated) or on CC's desktop (TBD). If on CC's desktop, native Python on Windows (not WSL2 — too fragile for a production service).
- **Kiosk client**: Pi 5 + touchscreen running Chromium fullscreen pointed at the server.
- **Info radiator**: Pi Zero 2 W + wall screen running Chromium fullscreen pointed at server /calendar endpoint.
- **Staff clients**: Existing Dell laptops, browser pointed at the server over WiFi.
- **Admin client**: CC's desktop, browser.

Recommendation: dedicated Pi 5 as server (always-on, single-purpose, Linux). Avoids dependency on CC's desktop being powered on. CC's desktop is just another browser client.

The C# argument was for a standalone Windows kiosk app. That architecture is no longer the plan. Flask/Python is the natural fit for a multi-client web app on Linux.

metadata.notes: WSL2 rejected — fragile networking, unreliable auto-start, not a server platform. Native Python on Windows is possible but a dedicated Pi server is cleaner. Decision point: dedicated server Pi vs CC desktop.

title: Q42: Information radiator: What events, calendar items, schedules, and brochure conte
description:
The information radiator is a separate wall-mounted display adjacent to the kiosk table. It shows calendar, events, service schedules, and replaces physical brochures with digital content.

**Hardware:** Pi Zero 2 W (~$20) connected via HDMI to a wall-mounted TV or monitor. The TV can be purchased from Costco (familiar vendor for EMO). A 32" TV is ~$100-150.

**Software:** Chromium in kiosk mode displaying a web page served by the Flask server (e.g., /calendar or /info-radiator). Staff update content through the admin interface on their laptops. The display auto-refreshes.

**Calendar syndication:** Calendar data stored in standard iCal format. Serves as both the info radiator content and a subscribable feed for mobile apps. Maintain once, display everywhere. Reference model: Trap Door Brewing events calendar.

**Content types:** Events, daily schedule, service hours, announcements, community resources. Staff manage through a simple CMS-like interface in the admin panel.

metadata.notes: Pi Zero 2 W is ~$15-20 for the board. Total info radiator cost: ~$120-170 (Pi Zero + cheap TV). Or if there is already a TV on the wall, just the Pi Zero. Chromecast/Fire Stick are alternatives but add a different platform to manage.

title: Q33: Open source or proprietary code? Open source (MIT): reusable, no lock-in, commun
description:
Dual-repo approach:

**Private repo** for EMO — their instance, their config, their issue tracker. CC and staff can file feature requests and bug reports via GitHub Issues. This gives them a structured feedback channel during development and after deployment.

**Open source repo** (upstream or adjacent) — the reusable platform, stripped of EMO-specific config and data. Portfolio piece. MIT or similar license. Other organizations and projects (e.g., Trap Door Brewing mug club) would be different configurations of the same system.

The EMO private repo tracks their fork/config. The open source repo is the generalizable engine. Features developed for EMO that are broadly useful get upstreamed. EMO-specific customizations stay in their private repo.

This is personal software — purpose-built, not generalized to the max. But dove-tailed so the same codebase serves different deployments with different configs.

metadata.notes: Two repos: private (EMO) and public (portfolio/reusable). GitHub Issues as the feedback mechanism for CC/staff. Trap Door Brewing mug club is another potential deployment of the same platform.

title: Q36: When do you want the system operational? Is there a hard deadline (grant cycle,
description:
Timeline TBD — pending Andrea meeting and hardware ordering.

**Development workflow:**
1. Build on Firebase/GCP (user already has Firebase experience) with synthetic data clearly annotated as fake
2. Give EMO/CC access to the hosted beta — they explore, file issues, provide feedback via GitHub Issues
3. Iterate based on feedback until approved
4. Deploy locally to Raspberry Pis on-site once approved
5. No personal client data on the hosted beta — synthetic only

**Post-deployment updates:**
- Software updates deployable from the internet (OTA) with review and control by admin/staff
- Rollback safety — if an update breaks something, revert to previous known-good state
- Data integrity guaranteed across updates — updates never touch client data
- No personal client data leaves the premises. Only code/features flow in, never data flows out.
- Admin/staff must approve updates before they take effect

**Phase 2 stretch goals** (only if ahead of schedule and under budget):
- Light IT services for Chautauqua + possible second shelter location
- NetBox for hardware/network documentation
- Additional integration points (HOP Fastpass, etc.)

metadata.notes: Firebase for staging is pragmatic — user knows it, low friction. The key principle: synthetic data for beta, real data only on-premises. OTA updates are a feature, not a risk, if admin controls the gate.

title: Q38: Development access: Who is my primary point of contact at EMO? Best way to reach
description:
Miss Cobine (CC) is the primary point of contact at EMO.

**Feedback workflow:** GitHub Issues on a private repo. CC and relevant staff get GitHub accounts (or we create a simple issue submission flow if GitHub is too unfamiliar). Feature requests, bug reports, and feedback are tracked as issues.

**Beta access:** Hosted staging environment (Firebase/GCP) with synthetic data. CC and staff can explore the system from their laptops/phones before anything is deployed on-site. This de-risks deployment — they know what they are getting before hardware arrives.

**During development:** CC available for questions. Communication channel TBD (email, phone, text). GitHub Issues becomes the durable record of decisions and requests.

metadata.notes: GitHub Issues serves double duty: feedback channel AND decision audit trail. If CC is not GitHub-comfortable, a simple web form that creates issues behind the scenes would work.

title: Q33: Open source or proprietary code? Open source (MIT): reusable, no lock-in, commun
description:
Dual-repo approach:

**Private repo** for EMO — their instance, their config, their issue tracker. CC and staff can file feature requests and bug reports via GitHub Issues. This gives them a structured feedback channel during development and after deployment.

**Open source repo** (upstream or adjacent) — the reusable platform, stripped of EMO-specific config and data. Portfolio piece. MIT or similar license. Other organizations and projects (e.g., Trap Door Brewing mug club) would be different configurations of the same system.

The EMO private repo tracks their fork/config. The open source repo is the generalizable engine. Features developed for EMO that are broadly useful get upstreamed. EMO-specific customizations stay in their private repo.

This is personal software — purpose-built, not generalized to the max. But dove-tailed so the same codebase serves different deployments with different configs.

metadata.notes: Two repos: private (EMO) and public (portfolio/reusable). GitHub Issues as the feedback mechanism for CC/staff. Trap Door Brewing mug club is another potential deployment of the same platform.

title: Q36: When do you want the system operational? Is there a hard deadline (grant cycle,
description:
Timeline TBD — pending Andrea meeting and hardware ordering.

**Development workflow:**
1. Build on Firebase/GCP (user already has Firebase experience) with synthetic data clearly annotated as fake
2. Give EMO/CC access to the hosted beta — they explore, file issues, provide feedback via GitHub Issues
3. Iterate based on feedback until approved
4. Deploy locally to Raspberry Pis on-site once approved
5. No personal client data on the hosted beta — synthetic only

**Post-deployment updates:**
- Software updates deployable from the internet (OTA) with review and control by admin/staff
- Rollback safety — if an update breaks something, revert to previous known-good state
- Data integrity guaranteed across updates — updates never touch client data
- No personal client data leaves the premises. Only code/features flow in, never data flows out.
- Admin/staff must approve updates before they take effect

**Phase 2 stretch goals** (only if ahead of schedule and under budget):
- Light IT services for Chautauqua + possible second shelter location
- NetBox for hardware/network documentation
- Additional integration points (HOP Fastpass, etc.)

metadata.notes: Firebase for staging is pragmatic — user knows it, low friction. The key principle: synthetic data for beta, real data only on-premises. OTA updates are a feature, not a risk, if admin controls the gate.

title: Q38: Development access: Who is my primary point of contact at EMO? Best way to reach
description:
Miss Cobine (CC) is the primary point of contact at EMO.

**Feedback workflow:** GitHub Issues on a private repo. CC and relevant staff get GitHub accounts (or we create a simple issue submission flow if GitHub is too unfamiliar). Feature requests, bug reports, and feedback are tracked as issues.

**Beta access:** Hosted staging environment (Firebase/GCP) with synthetic data. CC and staff can explore the system from their laptops/phones before anything is deployed on-site. This de-risks deployment — they know what they are getting before hardware arrives.

**During development:** CC available for questions. Communication channel TBD (email, phone, text). GitHub Issues becomes the durable record of decisions and requests.

metadata.notes: GitHub Issues serves double duty: feedback channel AND decision audit trail. If CC is not GitHub-comfortable, a simple web form that creates issues behind the scenes would work.

title: Q36: When do you want the system operational? Is there a hard deadline (grant cycle,

3. Beta includes all role-based views:

   - /staff — staff view (client records, notes, service tracking)

4. CC and staff can log in with different roles, watch records update in real-time as synthetic clients check in, see service slots fill, watch daily counts update

- Admin/staff must approve updates before they take effect

- Additional integration points (HOP Fastpass, etc.)

title: Q5: How many clients per day, roughly? Average day? Busiest day? How many total uniq
description: Conservative estimate: dozens of clients per day at most. Annual extrapolation is still small — well under 1000 unique clients. Open question: ratio of unique people vs. repeat visit frequency. Neither dimension is large enough to affect development decisions (database design, scroll list performance, etc.). Will refine with real data once system is live.

metadata.notes: Scale is small enough that performance is not a concern. The interesting metric is visit frequency per client, which is operational insight, not a sizing constraint.

title: Q7: What reports do you run today? Daily counts? Monthly summaries? Who reads these
description: Julie runs a report — daily, we believe — for CAREWare (Ryan White). Opportunity to automate: schedule a job to transmit CAREWare data and report success/failure to staff (IT health check pattern). Other reports will be discovered as we move along. Once staff are in the beta system, they can request external data syncs via the issue tracker. This is an iterative discovery — we don't need to enumerate all reports before building.

metadata.notes: Follow up with Julie on exact CAREWare workflow. Automation of daily upload is a high-value feature. Other reports are additive — build the framework, add reports as needed.

title: Q9: Do you currently report to HMIS? Which regional CoC? Which HMIS vendor (Clarity,
description: RSR (Ryan White Services Report) is the confirmed reporting obligation for now. HMIS and CoC participation will be revisited with Andrea — not urgent. If EMO does report to HMIS, we need to understand which CoC and vendor to ensure data compatibility. But this does not block development.

metadata.notes: Andrea meeting will clarify HMIS. RSR/CAREWare is the primary integration. HMIS is additive if it exists.

title: Q11: What other reporting obligations exist? Funder-specific reports? Internal board
description: Not urgent. Known additional reporting: JOH (quarterly), CACFP (quarterly), and at least one other annual funding source. Full enumeration will happen as we engage staff through the beta. Issue tracker is the mechanism for discovering and requesting new report types.

metadata.notes: Reporting obligations are additive. Build the data model to support flexible reporting, discover specific reports iteratively.

title: Q13: What demographic fields are needed at registration? For each: is it required by
description: Demographic fields will be determined by reviewing the data dump from the existing system (Andrea meeting) and cross-referencing with known funder requirements (RSR, CACFP). Start with whatever fields exist in the current data, validate against funder requirements, add any gaps.

metadata.notes: Data dump schema is the starting point. Andrea meeting is the gate.

title: Q14: What contact and program-specific fields are needed? (Phone, email, emergency co
description: Deferred to after Andrea meeting. Begin with the schema of the existing data dump, then loop back for additional fields. Contact and program-specific fields will emerge from the data migration review.

metadata.notes: Depends on Q13/Andrea meeting. Sequential — data dump first, then gap analysis.

title: Q15: HIV status collection: Does your grant reporting require tracking HIV status at
description: Deferred to after Andrea meeting. HIV status collection depends on whether Ryan White grant requires individual-level tracking or just aggregate counts. Default recommendation: don't collect unless funder explicitly requires it (ORS 433.045).

metadata.notes: Andrea to confirm grant requirements.

title: Q17: What check-in fields should be captured at each visit? (Services used, check-out
description: Deferred to after Andrea meeting. Check-in fields per visit will be informed by the existing data dump schema. Begin with what exists, loop back for additions.

metadata.notes: Sequential with Q13-Q14. Data dump first.

title: Q20: Power situation: Are there outlets at the check-in location? Is the power reliab
description: Power is reliable — not a concern. WiFi coverage has historically been spotty (RF issue, not power). Recently mitigated with additional AP. There is a power/charging device station in the shelter. The architecture already accounts for local operation — the kiosk and server communicate on LAN, so brief WiFi drops only affect staff laptop access and the info radiator, not core check-in functionality.

metadata.notes: WiFi spottiness is an RF/coverage issue, mitigated by the new AP. Power is fine. Architecture is resilient to WiFi drops for the core check-in path.

title: Q26: Do you want spare hardware on hand? A second mini PC or spare AIO means same-day
description: CC (Chautauqua Cobine) deferred the spare hardware question to Andrea. Decision depends on final hardware choice and budget.

metadata.notes: Andrea meeting.

title: Q34: Who holds the database decryption password? Which staff member(s)? Where is it s
description: Deferred to Andrea. We will provide guidance on key custody best practices (at least two people, sealed envelope in a safe, documented succession plan). Andrea to confirm organizational policy.

metadata.notes: Section 8 policy questions go through Andrea. We provide technical guidance.

title: Q35: Data incident procedure: Does EMO have an existing data breach policy? If a USB
description: Deferred to Andrea. We will provide guidance on data breach procedures (encrypted drives mitigate impact, notification chain, Oregon breach notification requirements). Andrea to confirm if EMO has an existing policy.

metadata.notes: Section 8 policy questions go through Andrea.

title: Q39: Training: How many staff need training? On-site training at deployment? Written
description: Deferred. The beta system may serve as the primary feedback and training mechanism. A written guide should be short, succinct, and written for the right audience level (possibly non-technical staff). The guide could be hosted in the staff admin portal as a built-in help section — always available, always current.

metadata.notes: Written guide as in-app help is a good pattern. Audience: non-technical staff. Keep it short.

title: Q40: Testing: Can I deploy a test version at the center before going live? Is there a
description:
Beta site hosted (affordable). The beta includes a harness/umbrella that indicates it is a beta environment and provides navigation to all system views: info radiator (/calendar), check-in kiosk (/checkin), staff view (/staff), admin view (/admin). Staff can walk through each view or we can demonstrate live. Notes or a guided walkthrough can be provided.

CRITICAL ARCHITECTURAL CONSTRAINT: The beta harness/umbrella navigation is a beta-only feature. It must NOT leak into the deployed on-site code. The on-site deployment has separate devices for each view (kiosk Pi, info radiator Pi, staff laptops). The beta harness exists only to let reviewers see all views from one browser. Implementation: environment flag or build configuration that excludes the harness from production builds.

metadata.notes: The beta harness is an important UX for stakeholder review but must be architecturally separated from production code. Environment variable or build flag — not a runtime toggle.
